evidence: Dependency direction is strictly layered: tools/ → toolkit/ (core) → never reverse. Coupling matrix shows tools/ imports toolkit/ 34 times; toolkit/ imports tools/ 9 times (index.ts re-exports only — intentional facade).
src/toolkit/index.ts is a clean barrel re-export with documented public surface. The 9 back-edges from toolkit/ → tools/ are all from index.ts collecting tool exports — intentional, not a cycle.

types.ts (21 importers) and session.ts (11 importers) are legitimately high fan-in hub modules — they define foundational types and the session contract that all tools depend on. This is by design, not accidental coupling.
guards/ → toolkit/ is 7 edges, all pointing to types.ts and state-helpers.ts. tools/ → guards/ is 6 edges. No bidirectional edges between guards/ and tools/ — guards are purely downstream consumers.

connect.ts has two lazy dynamic imports pointing outside the toolkit boundary: `await import('../../lib/auth.js')` at lines 222 and 244. These cross from src/toolkit/tools/ up into src/lib/ — a different subsystem.

The BUNDLED_CATALOG_PATH in discover-sources.ts uses import.meta.url and string replacement to navigate to config/sources/catalog.json — a build-time path that hardcodes the location of an external config dir.

sdk-bridge.ts uses dynamic lazy import for '@kynesyslabs/demosdk/d402/client' (payD402) and '@kynesyslabs/demosdk/websdk' (getTxModule) — documented as intentional to prevent module-level side effects.

Module-level mutable cache in discover-sources.ts: `const catalogCache = new Map<string, Source[]>()` — shared across all uses of the module. Isolated to read-only catalog data (no writes by tools), low blast radius.

VALID_STATUSES and normalizeStatus in discover-sources.ts are flagged as deprecated (0 importers for VALID_STATUSES) but still present in the file. They're used internally by normalizeStatus which is still active, so the deprecation signal is partly inaccurate — but VALID_STATUSES itself is not imported externally.
No circular dependency cycles detected in import graph summary. All detected cycles would pass through the index.ts facade, not through tool-to-tool or guard-to-guard imports.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: connect.ts has two dynamic imports crossing from src/toolkit/tools/ into src/lib/auth.js — a boundary violation where a toolkit tool reaches into the broader app's lib layer. This is the primary structural issue. The module-level catalog cache in discover-sources.ts is a minor shared-mutable-state concern.

evidence: The toolkit directory structure maps cleanly to domain capabilities: tools/ (user-facing operations), guards/ (invariant enforcement), core files (types, session, schemas, state-store, sdk-bridge, url-validator). Each directory has a single coherent purpose.

index.ts exports exactly the public API — tools, types, session, state-store, guards, schemas. The export surface is comprehensive but organized by capability group with clear header comments. A new engineer can find any entry point without searching.

The three-layer pattern (connect → session → tools → guards) is consistent across all 10 tools. Every tool accepts DemosSession, returns ToolResult<T>, validates input via schemas, and calls guards before side effects. This pattern is reliably applied.
CLAUDE.md accurately describes the toolkit: '10 tools, 6 guards, typed contracts, DemosSession, FileStateStore, SDK bridge, SSRF validator'. The runtime matches the documentation.

discoverSources() accepts `session: DemosSession | null` — the only tool that makes session optional. This is documented ('Read-only, no session required') but creates a visible inconsistency in the public API surface. A new engineer reading the API would ask why this one is different.

The guards directory exposes both old-style (checkDedup/recordPublish) and new-style (checkAndRecordDedup) functions. The backward-compat wrappers are documented inline but double the public guard surface unnecessarily.

pay.ts contains an inline Zod schema (D402RequirementSchema) for validating 402 response bodies. All other input validation lives in schemas.ts — this is a small ownership violation: schema definitions are split between the dedicated schemas file and a tool file.
The toolkit is scoped entirely to src/toolkit/ — 24 files, 3138 LOC. The package_size_census shows 100% of tracked files under src/. This is a narrow, focused codebase with no sprawl.
high_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: discoverSources() nullable session parameter breaks API uniformity. The backward-compat guard dual-surface adds noise to the public index.ts without clear external need. D402RequirementSchema defined inside pay.ts rather than schemas.ts is a minor ownership inconsistency.

evidence: All files use camelCase for functions and variables, PascalCase for types/interfaces/classes — consistent with TypeScript conventions. 141 function names sampled, all follow the same convention.
File naming is uniformly kebab-case across all three directories (tools/, guards/, toolkit/). No outliers found.
All modules use named exports exclusively — no default exports anywhere in the toolkit. Export pattern is 100% consistent across 24 files.

Guards follow a shared behavioral protocol: type-define local state types, import stateKey/loadState/checkAndAppend/GUARD_LOCK_TTL_MS from state-helpers.ts, return DemosError | null for check functions. 5 of 7 guards follow this pattern exactly.

backoff.ts is a structural outlier in guards/: it has no local state types, does not import loadState or GUARD_LOCK_TTL_MS, and follows the withBackoff(operation) async wrapper pattern instead of the check/record pattern. This is correct — backoff is a different kind of guard — but the mechanical scanner flagged it as missing shared patterns.
state-helpers.ts is also flagged as an outlier in guards/ for not importing GUARD_LOCK_TTL_MS/loadState — but it IS the file that exports them. This is a false positive from the scanner.

The guards directory has two public API styles: old-style pair (checkX/recordX) and new unified (checkAndRecordX). dedup-guard.ts and write-rate-limit.ts expose both: checkDedup/recordPublish (old) alongside checkAndRecordDedup/checkAndRecordWrite (new). tip-spend-cap.ts exposes both checkTipSpendCap/recordTip (old) and checkAndRecordTip (new). This dual surface is documented as 'backward compat' but presents convention inconsistency — callers must choose which API to use.

Tools follow consistent verb-first naming: checkWriteRateLimit, checkDedup, checkPaySpendCap, recordWrite, recordPublish, recordTip, recordPayment. The only outlier is checkAndRecordX which merges two operations but uses a different naming style than either the check or record convention.

discover-sources.ts defines a local SourceStatus type alias (line 100) that duplicates the exported SourceStatus type from types.ts. This creates a convention outlier: one type defined in two places with the same name but different visibility.

verify.ts imports sleep from guards/state-helpers.ts — a tool importing a utility from the guards layer. The sleep utility's placement in state-helpers.ts is a mild ownership mismatch (sleep has nothing to do with guard state), but it's a shared file pattern.

The `domain` parameter in ScanOptions is imported by scan.ts but scan.ts does not use it to filter (it passes to fetchFeed, which ignores it in practice — domain filtering not yet implemented). The unused import in scan.ts is flagged by the scanner.
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: Dual guard API surface (old checkX/recordX + new checkAndRecordX) creates API choice ambiguity for consumers. Local SourceStatus type in discover-sources.ts duplicates the exported type. These are visible convention inconsistencies in the public surface.

evidence: The toolkit has a documented two-strategy model: tools return ToolResult<T> (ok/err envelope); internal helpers and SDK bridge throw. This layering is intentional and consistent with the 'Result in core, throw in CLI' pattern the rubric permits.
withToolWrapper catches all thrown exceptions from tool implementations and converts them to typed DemosError — this is the single error translation boundary. The pattern works correctly.
Guards uniformly return DemosError | null — no guards throw. This is consistent across all 7 guard files.
connect.ts throws DemosError directly (not wrapped in ToolResult) — intentional because connect() is a lifecycle function, not a toolkit tool. This is documented behavior but visible inconsistency.

sdk-bridge.ts throws raw Error objects (not DemosError) from attestDahr, publishHivePost, transferDem, and signAndBroadcast. withToolWrapper wraps these into DemosError on catch. However, payD402 has its own catch block at line 288 that re-throws either the original (if D402SettlementResult-shaped) or a new Error — the re-throw is NOT converted to DemosError before leaving sdk-bridge, relying on withToolWrapper in pay.ts to handle it. This is a fragile two-step conversion.

publish.ts: executePublishPipeline() throws demosError() directly (line 92: `throw demosError('INVALID_INPUT', ...)`) and also throws plain `new Error()` from bridge.attestDahr (bridged through). withToolWrapper catches both and handles them differently — isDemosError check distinguishes DemosError thrown intentionally from plain Error.

scan.ts inner catch (line 38) silently swallows the Skill Dojo fallback error with `catch { /* both failed */ }` — the original local error (localErr) is partially preserved in the outer catch message, but the Skill Dojo failure is completely discarded with no context.

verify.ts: the inner catch at line 88 in checkConfirmation wraps the error into a new Error with added context ('Verification requires SuperColony API...'). This is good — context is preserved. However the outer verify() function has a separate `return err(...)` at line 59 after the loop that's a dead code path — the loop always exits via return on the final attempt.

discover-sources.ts: loadCatalog() calls JSON.parse(raw) at line 80 without try/catch — raw is from readFile and could be malformed JSON. The outer try/catch in discoverSources() at line 43 catches this and wraps it properly. However, the JSON.parse is in the inner helper and the outer catch is in a different function scope — this is the pattern flagged by the 'security' detector ('JSON.parse without try/catch').

tip-spend-cap.ts checkAndRecordTip has a logic order issue: the per-tip max check (line 36: `if (amount > policy.maxPerTip)`) happens BEFORE the isFinite/positive check (line 44). If amount is NaN or Infinity, the first check (NaN > 10 = false) passes, and the second check catches it — but this relies on comparison-with-NaN behavior being falsy, which is correct but non-obvious. The more defensive order would be: validate first, then apply policy checks.
pay-spend-cap.ts has the same order issue: line 34 checks `amount > policy.maxPerCall` before line 42 validates `isFinite(amount)`. Same NaN-comparison dependency.
error_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The silent Skill Dojo error swallow in scan.ts is a concrete information loss. The JSON.parse without local try/catch in discover-sources.ts loadCatalog() is a confirmed vulnerability (relying on an outer catch in a different function scope). The NaN-before-isFinite validation order in tip-spend-cap and pay-spend-cap is non-obvious and order-dependent. These aren't catastrophic but create predictability gaps.

pay.ts is the most complex file (320 LOC, 9 named steps) — but each step is logically distinct (idempotency check, SSRF, spend cap, parse 402, payee validation, amount guard, settle, record spend, retry). No unrelated concerns mixed in. The step comments (Step 1-9) make the sequence readable.

fetchWithValidatedRedirects in pay.ts uses a while(true) loop with explicit redirectsFollowed counter and max-hops guard — the infinite loop pattern requires reading carefully but the guard is correct and the purpose is clear

verify.ts has a control flow issue: the retry loop (lines 29-56) uses both 'continue' and a post-loop fallback err() (lines 59-62). The post-loop return is dead code — the loop runs attempt=0..3 (4 iterations for RETRY_DELAYS_MS.length=3), and on the last attempt both catch branch and the non-confirmed branch return directly. The outer fallback is never reached but looks load-bearing.

tip.ts resolves the post author by fetching the full feed and doing a linear scan (lines 38-55) — this works but the approach of fetching 50 posts to resolve one author is implicit, side-effectful, and not surfaced in the function signature or docstring
scan.ts's identifyOpportunities function (lines 49-62) uses a hardcoded reaction threshold of 5 and score of 0.7 — magic numbers with no named constants or explanation
checkBlockedIpv4 in url-validator.ts is a long chain of if-returns covering 9 IP ranges — each check is a single condition with an informative reason string. Not deep nesting; direct and readable.

connect.ts's detectContainer() reads three files in sequence: /.dockerenv, /proc/1/cgroup, /proc/mounts — each wrapped in try/catch. This is correctly structured sequential fallback, not defensive sprawl; the comment explains WSL2 detection.

backoff.ts uses non-null assertions (lastResult!) at the end of withBackoff() — the assertions are correct (loop runs at least once) but the type could be narrowed by initializing lastResult as the first iteration result directly
sdk-bridge.ts publishHivePost has a 6-fallback txHash extraction chain (lines 251-256) to handle multiple SDK response shapes — this is necessary given SDK instability but visually complex
stateKey() in state-helpers.ts is a 2-line function that returns a string — minimal and precise. sleep() is a standard 1-liner. Both are appropriately not extracted further.

The 'while (true)' in fetchWithValidatedRedirects is bounded by redirectsFollowed >= MAX_REDIRECT_HOPS guard and by 'return response' on non-redirect status. The loop termination conditions require active inspection to confirm correctness.
In tip.ts, the linear feed scan for author resolution (O(n) over 50 posts) with no pagination or targeted lookup is a functional but imprecise approach — works for small feeds, fragile for deep posts

parseWallet in connect.ts handles 3 wallet formats (JSON with address, JSON with mnemonic, key=value) via a try/catch + instanceof chain. Each format path returns early or throws. The flow is somewhat convoluted but each path is distinct and commented.
withToolWrapper pattern in tool-wrapper.ts cleanly extracts cross-cutting concerns (timing, touch, onToolCall, error wrapping) with a single consistent calling convention across 7+ tools

Mixed sync/async detected by scan: src/toolkit/tools/connect.ts, src/toolkit/guards/pay-receipt-log.ts, src/toolkit/guards/state-helpers.ts, src/toolkit/tools/tool-wrapper.ts all appear in sync_async_mix list — tool-wrapper.ts has localProvenance which is sync, not a public API concern
discoverSources exports are named 'discoverSources' (camelCase) but the DiscoverSourcesOptions has matchThreshold field which is not used within discoverSources itself — it is a dead option field

No route handlers, HTTP middleware, or RLS tables exist — this is a toolkit, not a web server. Auth consistency must be evaluated at the trust boundary layer: session gating, SSRF prevention, spend-cap enforcement, and credential handling.

All write/chain tools (publish, attest, tip, pay, react, scan, verify) uniformly require a DemosSession parameter. discoverSources accepts `session: DemosSession | null` — explicitly documented as a read-only tool that requires no auth. This is intentional and clearly documented.

SSRF validation via validateUrl() is consistently applied at all three tools that accept caller-controlled URLs: attest.ts (line 30), pay.ts (line 59), and publish.ts executePublishPipeline (line 88). In pay.ts, each redirect hop also re-validates (line 267). The three sibling URL-accepting tools all apply the same control.

scan.ts, react.ts, verify.ts, tip.ts use only relative paths via bridge.apiCall() — absolute URL injection is blocked at the bridge boundary (sdk-bridge.ts line 173-174). These tools have no SSRF risk because they never accept caller-controlled URLs.

sdk-bridge.ts apiCall() (line 185): Bearer token is auto-attached when authToken !== AUTH_PENDING_TOKEN. All API-calling tools rely on this single enforcement point rather than managing tokens individually — this is the correct pattern.

CRITICAL: urlAllowlist is defined in ConnectOptions (types.ts line 108), stored in DemosSession (session.ts line 36), but never passed to validateUrl(). UrlValidationOptions has no allowlist parameter. A caller who configures urlAllowlist gets no filtering effect — the control is wired but dead.

CRITICAL: connect.ts authenticateFallback() (line 248) returns the string literal 'auth-pending' when no cached token exists. AUTH_PENDING_TOKEN in sdk-bridge.ts is '__AUTH_PENDING__'. These two strings are different. The apiCall guard checks `authToken !== AUTH_PENDING_TOKEN` (i.e., !== '__AUTH_PENDING__'). For address-only wallets with no auth cache, the session receives 'auth-pending' as its token, which passes the guard and causes apiCall to emit 'Bearer auth-pending' — an invalid token — to every SuperColony API call.

connect.ts auth failure path (line 224-227): when ensureAuth() throws, authToken is set to AUTH_PENDING_TOKEN and execution continues, creating a degraded session. The degraded-session pattern is intentional and documented, but combined with the sentinel mismatch in authenticateFallback, the auth posture for read-only wallet sessions is unpredictable.
Spend-cap guards (tip-spend-cap, pay-spend-cap, write-rate-limit) are wallet-scoped via stateKey() and persist across session restarts. Consistent enforcement across all spending tools.

pay.ts payee trust gate (line 140-148): session.payPolicy.requirePayeeApproval is checked before any settlement. The trusted payees check on line 141 does `const trusted = session.payPolicy.trustedPayees ?? []` — this ?? incomplete_migration.dimension_character: The codebase shows one genuine incomplete migration: three guard modules introduced a unified checkAndRecord* API to allow atomic check+record in a single lock acquisition, but the old separate check/record functions were retained as 'backward compat' wrappers and the new API was never exported from index.ts. This means external consumers are locked into the two-call pattern and cannot benefit from the atomic API without importing internals. The mechanical scan signals for VALID_STATUSES, normalizeStatus, Source, and 'active' are false positives — those symbols are actively used within their files, not deprecated.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: At 80 ('solid but uneven') a developer encounters old and new patterns coexisting without clarity on which to follow. That description fits exactly: three guard files have a new checkAndRecord* API that is unreachable via the public index.ts, forcing callers to use the old split API even though the unified one exists. This is real migration friction, but it is contained to three guards and has zero impact on correctness — the wrappers are functionally correct. The absence of any other migration debt (no mixed JS/TS, no stale TODOs, no deprecated-but-called symbols) keeps the score from falling further. 78.0 reflects one concrete coexistence issue in a small, focused subsystem.

package_organization.dimension_character: The three-directory layout (toolkit/, guards/, tools/) maps precisely to the three distinct responsibilities: shared contracts and session, safety guards, and tool implementations. Coupling flows unidirectionally without reciprocal edges. File counts are appropriate and every directory name accurately predicts its contents. The structure is small enough (24 files) that navigation is intuitive, and the barrel index.ts provides a single entry point without obscuring internals.
package_organization.score_rationale: At the 90 anchor ('mostly coherent with minor placement outliers'), this codebase meets or exceeds expectations. The coupling matrix shows clean layering, no stragglers, and no affinity mismatches. The only minor navigability question is whether tool-wrapper.ts (a shared utility used by 7 of 10 tools) should be at toolkit/ rather than tools/ — but since all its importers are tools, it is not a structural violation. Scoring at 91.5 rather than 95+ because of this single mild placement ambiguity and the tight 24-file scope which limits the evidence base for assessing navigability at scale.

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: The toolkit demonstrates strong discipline around initialization coupling: all SDK imports are lazy, no environment variable reads occur at module scope, and the Zod schema constants are pure computations. The main gap is two module-level caches (_isContainer and catalogCache) that create shared state across test runs, and DEFAULT_STATE_DIR which captures homedir() at module load. These are isolated cases with clear rationale and runtime mitigations (overrides via constructor/options), not systemic patterns.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: At 90 ('mostly stable with limited boot-order fragility') a developer would trust the import model with limited exceptions. The toolkit earns this range: lazy SDK imports are explicitly the design goal, and the three module-level singletons are documented or mitigated. The score is pulled to 87 rather than 90 because catalogCache is shared across tests without a flush mechanism, and DEFAULT_STATE_DIR captures homedir() at import time which is the exact pattern flagged in the initialization_coupling rubric. These are genuine initialization coupling patterns, but their practical impact is low due to available overrides.

design_coherence.dimension_character: The toolkit shows strong structural discipline: a consistent withToolWrapper pipeline pattern, well-scoped helper extraction in pay.ts and state-helpers.ts, and focused single-responsibility files throughout. The main tension points are connect.ts mixing 5 concerns in one function body and sdk-bridge.ts's apiCall() silently swallowing network errors into a response shape that callers cannot easily distinguish from HTTP errors.
design_coherence.score_rationale: Score 78.5 reflects a codebase that is structurally sound with consistent patterns and good extraction discipline, but has two notable design issues: connect.ts accumulating 5 responsibilities and sdk-bridge.ts silent error conversion. The flagged 'high cyclomatic complexity' files (url-validator.ts, discover-sources.ts) are false positives — their branching is inherent to their domain. The 'monster function' in pay.ts is a sequential protocol implementation with a single clear responsibility.

contract_coherence.dimension_character: The toolkit has strong structural contract discipline — 10 tools uniformly return ToolResult<T>, error creation is centralized via demosError(), and session lifecycle methods are consistently honest about what they do and throw. However, two concrete contract violations undermine trust at the point of use: PublishDraft.attestUrl is typed optional but required at runtime, and verify() returns a hardcoded blockHeight: 1 that appears meaningful but is fabricated.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: Score 74.0 reflects a codebase with a correct and consistent macro-level contract (ToolResult<T> everywhere, typed error codes, no mixed throw/return within the module) but two runtime contract violations that directly mislead callers. The optional/required mismatch on attestUrl is a type annotation lie that TypeScript's type system cannot catch and will cause silent runtime failures. The hardcoded blockHeight:1 creates false data that callers may log or relay on-chain.

logic_clarity.dimension_character: The toolkit's control flow is generally direct and intention-revealing — guard chains, retry loops, and protocol steps are clearly sequenced without redundant branching. Two concrete logic defects exist: a dead code path after verify()'s for-loop (unreachable return), and an inverted validation check order in pay-spend-cap.ts that allows invalid amounts to bypass the isFinite check.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: Score 76.5 reflects solid overall logic clarity with no egregious branching issues, but two correctness problems: dead code after the verify retry loop (indicating the loop termination condition was not fully reasoned through) and a guard ordering issue in pay-spend-cap.ts where the maxPerCall check fires before the isFinite/positive check, which can produce misleading error codes for invalid input.

type_safety.dimension_character: The toolkit's public API surface is well-typed with no any escapes and schema-to-interface sync assertions that prevent drift. Type safety degrades at SDK integration boundaries in sdk-bridge.ts, where the Demos SDK lacks declared types for web2.createDahr(), sendTransaction(), and transfer() — forcing pervasive as any casts. The non-SDK-forced issues are: bare Function parameters for txModule (no call-site checking), any-typed cache variables, a duplicate SourceStatus type in discover-sources.ts, and the any-return on the demos field in connectSdk(). Together these create a codebase where the developer can trust public API types but must treat sdk-bridge.ts as a type-unsafe layer.
type_safety.score_rationale: Score 71.0. The public surface is genuinely strong (a high-confidence positive). The sdk-bridge.ts any saturation accounts for substantial type coverage loss at the most security-relevant integration point — the SDK boundary where tokens and chain operations live. Some of this is SDK-forced, but the bare Function typing for txModule and the any cache variables are not SDK-forced and could be improved. The duplicate SourceStatus type creates a silent divergence risk. This combination places the codebase in the 70-75 range: a developer can trust API types but must independently verify runtime behavior in sdk-bridge.ts.

issues.suggestion: Replace each recordX() with a call to checkAndAppend passing record=true-equivalent: pass newEntry and a no-op check that always returns null. This eliminates 4 separate lock+load+prune+push+store sequences. Example for recordPublish: call checkAndAppend(store, key, DEFAULT_STATE, DAY_MS, () => null, { timestamp: Date.now(), hash }).
evidence: dedup-guard.ts recordPublish(): load state, filter dayAgo, push entry, store — 6 lines
write-rate-limit.ts recordWrite(): load state, filter dayAgo, push entry, store — 6 lines
tip-spend-cap.ts recordTip(): load state, filter dayAgo, push entry, store — 6 lines
pay-spend-cap.ts recordPayment(): load state, filter dayAgo, push entry, store — 6 lines
checkAndAppend in state-helpers.ts already does exactly this with pruneWindowMs, entry append, and lock

issues.summary: SourceStatus type and VALID_STATUSES set re-declared locally in discover-sources.ts despite canonical export in types.ts
issues.suggestion: Import SourceStatus from '../types.js' and delete the local re-declaration. Replace VALID_STATUSES with a type-safe lookup: const VALID_STATUSES = new Set<SourceStatus>(['active','degraded','quarantined','stale','deprecated','archived']). This is a single-file change.
evidence: discover-sources.ts line 100: 'type SourceStatus = "active" | "degraded" | ...' — zero importers
types.ts line 237: 'export type SourceStatus = "active" | "degraded" | ...' — same union, exported
VALID_STATUSES Set at line 102 duplicates the canonical SourceStatus values as a runtime set

issues.summary: Unified checkAndRecord* API exists in guards but is not exported — callers forced to use backward-compat wrappers
issues.suggestion: Complete the migration: add checkAndRecordWrite, checkAndRecordDedup, and checkAndRecordTip to index.ts exports alongside or replacing the backward-compat wrappers. Once no internal callers reference the old API, remove the wrapper functions. The migration is already done in each guard file — only index.ts needs updating.
evidence: write-rate-limit.ts: checkAndRecordWrite() is the unified API (combines check+record atomically), but index.ts exports only checkWriteRateLimit (line 82) and recordWrite (line 83) — both labeled '(backward compat)'
dedup-guard.ts: checkAndRecordDedup() is the unified API; index.ts exports only checkDedup and recordPublish (lines 95-98)
tip-spend-cap.ts: checkAndRecordTip() is the unified API; index.ts exports only checkTipSpendCap and recordTip (lines 86-88)
External consumers cannot use the atomic check+record operation without importing directly from guard files, bypassing the stable public API surface

issues.summary: Module-level catalogCache in discover-sources.ts has no flush mechanism — tests sharing the same module instance cannot isolate catalog contents
issues.suggestion: Export a clearCatalogCache() function for test use: 'export function clearCatalogCache() { catalogCache.clear(); }'. This is a single-line addition that lets tests reset state between runs without restructuring the production cache logic.
evidence: Line 18: 'const catalogCache = new Map<string, Source[]>()' — module-level singleton
Line 76-77: cache is checked and populated without any TTL or invalidation
Tests that load a custom catalog in one test and a different one in another within the same process will see the first cached value on subsequent calls to the same path

issues.summary: apiCall() catch block silently converts network exceptions to ok:false response — callers cannot distinguish DNS failure from HTTP 500
issues.suggestion: Re-throw or surface as a DemosError(NETWORK_ERROR) so that callers can distinguish retryable network failures from non-retryable HTTP errors. If apiCall must never throw, add a third field (e.g., networkError: true) to ApiCallResult.
evidence: Lines 199-201: catch(err) { return { ok: false, status: 0, data: (err as Error).message }; }
status: 0 is the only signal that this was a network error vs an HTTP error; callers checking result.ok will treat both identically
The existing DemosError system has a NETWORK_ERROR code with retryable:true that is the correct carrier for this case

issues.summary: connect() mixes 5 distinct responsibilities in one function body: security checks, credential parsing, SDK connection, auth, session construction
issues.suggestion: Extract the security + file reading into a helper (e.g., readSecureWalletFile) and the session construction into a helper (e.g., buildSession). This would leave connect() as a readable orchestration of 3-4 clearly named steps rather than 5 interleaved responsibilities.
evidence: Lines 47-55: symlink check and TOCTOU prevention
Lines 58-77: file mode enforcement with container detection branch
Lines 80-81: wallet credential parsing (3 format branches in parseWallet)
Lines 83-88: SDK connection and auth via connectSdk
Lines 90-112: session construction with 12 options mapped

issues.reasoning: High cyclomatic complexity from mechanical detector but each branch handles a distinct catalog shape or field fallback. This is inherent multi-format normalization, not design fragmentation.
Monster function detector fired on LOC, but the function has one responsibility (D402 payment flow) with helpers already extracted. Length is justified by the protocol's sequential steps.
Async stub that throws — the async signature is forward-compatible with the planned implementation. Not a design problem.
IP range enumeration cannot be meaningfully refactored — each range has a distinct semantic name. A data-driven table would not reduce complexity and would obscure the named ranges.

The 6 files do not share a common anti-pattern. The backward-compat delegation wrappers (async returning async call result) are idiomatic TypeScript. The async stub is intentional. No systemic fix warranted.

issues.summary: PublishDraft.attestUrl typed optional but throws at runtime if absent — type annotation lies to callers
issues.suggestion: Change PublishDraft.attestUrl from optional to required (remove '?'). If there is a genuine future use case for omitting it, make that an explicit second interface (PublishDraftWithoutAttestation) with a comment explaining when attestation is skippable. Do not let the type promise something the runtime rejects.
evidence: types.ts line 129: attestUrl?: string  — optional by type
publish.ts line 84: throw new Error('PublishDraft.attestUrl is required') — runtime required
schemas.ts line 96: attestUrl: nonEmptyString.optional()  — schema also says optional
schemas.ts lines 10-14 documents this as 'intentional' but the type annotation still communicates the wrong contract to callers

issues.summary: verify() returns blockHeight: 1 hardcoded — callers receive fabricated data as if it were real chain data
issues.suggestion: Remove the hardcoded blockHeight entirely: return { confirmed: found } when found is true. If blockHeight is available from the feed API response in the future, populate it then. Do not return a placeholder number in a field that implies real chain data.
evidence: verify.ts line 87: return { confirmed: found, blockHeight: found ? 1 : undefined }
VerifyResult.blockHeight?: number implies actual chain block height
Any caller logging or relaying this value on-chain would surface false provenance data

issues.summary: SdkBridge.signAndBroadcast() exposed in interface but no tool uses it and txData shape is untyped unknown
issues.suggestion: Remove signAndBroadcast() from the SdkBridge interface if no tool uses it, or document what txData shape is expected and add at least one typed overload. Dead interface methods on the public contract confuse consumers about the intended usage surface.
evidence: sdk-bridge.ts line 71: signAndBroadcast(txData: unknown): Promise<{ hash: string }>
No tool in src/toolkit/tools/ calls session.getBridge().signAndBroadcast()
txData: unknown provides no guidance to callers on what to pass

issues.summary: verify() has unreachable return err() after the retry for-loop — loop always returns on final iteration
issues.suggestion: Remove lines 59-62. The for-loop condition `attempt <= RETRY_DELAYS_MS.length` already covers all retry attempts and the final failure case is handled inside the catch on the last iteration. If TypeScript requires a non-void return, restructure the loop to `attempt < RETRY_DELAYS_MS.length` with explicit exhausted handling after the loop.
evidence: for loop: attempt 0..RETRY_DELAYS_MS.length inclusive (0,1,2,3) — 4 iterations
On attempt=3 (last): try block returns ok({confirmed}) or ok({confirmed:false}); catch block returns err(CONFIRM_TIMEOUT). Loop always exits via return.
Lines 59-62: return err(demosError('CONFIRM_TIMEOUT', 'Verification exhausted all retries', true), ...) is never reached.

issues.summary: pay-spend-cap.ts checks amount > maxPerCall before isFinite/positive validation — inverted guard order
issues.suggestion: Move the isFinite/positive check (lines 42-44) to before the maxPerCall check (line 34). Validate that amount is a valid number before comparing it against policy limits.
evidence: Line 34: if (amount > policy.maxPerCall) return SPEND_LIMIT — fires before isFinite check
Line 42: if (!Number.isFinite(amount) || amount <= 0) return INVALID_INPUT — second check
If amount = -5: -5 > maxPerCall is false → passes line 34 → line 42 catches it (INVALID_INPUT). Correct result but wrong code path — the per-call max fires first.
If amount = Infinity: Infinity > maxPerCall is true → returns SPEND_LIMIT. Correct by accident — but the real error is INVALID_INPUT (non-finite), not SPEND_LIMIT.
Callers get SPEND_LIMIT for Infinity input, which suggests they should raise their maxSpend, not that the input is invalid.

issues.summary: txModule parameter typed with bare Function — call signatures are unchecked at every use site
issues.suggestion: Replace bare Function with explicit call signatures: `{ store: (data: Uint8Array, sdk: unknown) => Promise<unknown>; confirm: (tx: unknown, sdk: unknown) => Promise<unknown>; broadcast: (validity: unknown, sdk: unknown) => Promise<unknown> }`. Using unknown for SDK-typed parameters is still safer than Function — it forces an explicit cast at use sites rather than silently accepting any argument.
evidence: sdk-bridge.ts line 105: txModule?: { store: Function; confirm: Function; broadcast: Function }
Actual call sites: tx.store(encoded, demos) line 244, tx.confirm(storeTx, demos) line 245, tx.broadcast(validity, demos) line 246
Function type provides no parameter or return type constraints — passing wrong argument types compiles silently

issues.summary: cachedTxModule and cachedD402Client typed as any — type information lost on cache-hit path
issues.suggestion: Use unknown instead of any for cache variables. `let cachedTxModule: unknown = txModule ?? null`. At the single use site `const tx = txModule ?? await getTxModule()`, the existing narrowing (checking that tx exists before calling tx.store) is already present — switching to unknown forces the existing code path through explicit narrowing rather than implicitly allowing all property access.
evidence: sdk-bridge.ts line 108: let cachedTxModule: any = txModule ?? null
sdk-bridge.ts line 109: let cachedD402Client: any = null
After dynamic import: cachedTxModule = DemosTransactions (a static class object), cachedD402Client = new D402Client(demos) — both have known shapes post-import

issues.summary: discover-sources.ts defines a local SourceStatus type that exactly duplicates the exported type in types.ts
issues.suggestion: Remove the local SourceStatus type definition on line 100 of discover-sources.ts and add SourceStatus to the import on line 12: `import type { DiscoverSourcesOptions, DiscoverSourcesResult, Source, SourceStatus, ToolResult } from '../types.js'`.
evidence: types.ts line 237: export type SourceStatus = 'active' | 'degraded' | 'quarantined' | 'stale' | 'deprecated' | 'archived'
discover-sources.ts line 100: type SourceStatus = 'active' | 'degraded' | 'quarantined' | 'stale' | 'deprecated' | 'archived' (local, unexported)
discover-sources.ts imports Source from types.ts (line 12) but not SourceStatus — the duplicate was introduced to avoid widening the import

normalizeStatus() uses the local type for its return annotation; if types.ts adds a new status value, normalizeStatus will silently return 'active' for unrecognized values instead of passing them through

issues.summary: connectSdk() return type has demos: any — Demos instance loses type information at connection site
issues.suggestion: In connect.ts, add `import type { Demos } from '@kynesyslabs/demosdk/websdk'` and change the connectSdk() return type to `Promise<{ demos: Demos; address: string; authToken: string }>`. The lazy import pattern does not prevent using the type at the signature level — type imports are compile-time only.
evidence: connect.ts line 187: ): Promise<{ demos: any; address: string; authToken: string }>
sdk-bridge.ts line 11: import type { Demos } from '@kynesyslabs/demosdk/websdk' — the Demos type IS importable
createSdkBridge() in sdk-bridge.ts accepts `demos: Demos` as its first parameter (line 100), so the type exists and is usable
The any typing on demos means all downstream uses of the Demos instance lose IDE support and compiler checking

add.header: Strict three-layer dependency direction enforced
add.description: tools/ → core/ → guards/ with no bidirectional edges. The 9 back-edges from toolkit/ to tools/ are all index.ts re-exports — an intentional facade, not a cycle. Any senior engineer reading the coupling matrix would confirm the direction is clean.

add.header: Hub modules are architecturally justified
add.description: types.ts (21 importers) and session.ts (11 importers) have high fan-in by design — they define the typed contracts and session handle that every tool must use. This is not accidental coupling; it reflects the correct information-hiding boundary.

add.header: connect.ts crosses toolkit boundary into app lib layer
add.description: Two dynamic imports reach into src/lib/auth.js from within the toolkit, violating the framework-agnostic contract. The toolkit should not depend on project-specific auth infrastructure.

add.header: Uniform three-layer tool pattern applied consistently
add.description: All 10 tools follow: accept DemosSession, validate input via schemas, run guards before side effects, return ToolResult<T> via withToolWrapper. This pattern is reliably applied and a new engineer can trust it holds everywhere.

add.header: Index.ts public surface organized by capability group
add.description: index.ts groups exports under labeled sections (Core Types, Session, State Store, Tools, Guards, Schemas) with matching header comments. The toolkit's responsibility is immediately navigable from the barrel.

add.header: discoverSources nullable session breaks API uniformity
add.description: All other tools require DemosSession; discoverSources accepts null. This is a legitimate design (read-only, no SDK needed) but creates API inconsistency visible at the index.ts export level.

add.header: Uniform named exports and kebab-case file naming
add.description: All 24 production files use named exports exclusively and kebab-case filenames. No default exports, no naming convention drift. A developer can apply the file-naming and export pattern from any file and trust it holds everywhere.

add.header: Guard behavioral protocol consistent across 5 of 7 files
add.description: The check/record guard pattern (import stateKey/loadState/checkAndAppend, return DemosError|null) is applied consistently across dedup, write-rate, tip-spend, pay-spend, and pay-receipt guards. backoff.ts and state-helpers.ts are structural outliers by intent, not convention drift.

add.header: Dual guard API surface without deprecation signals
add.description: Old checkX/recordX pairs and new unified checkAndRecordX functions coexist in three guard modules without deprecation markers. Callers cannot determine the preferred API from the public surface alone.

add.header: Two-layer error model clearly defined and mostly consistent
add.description: Guards return DemosError|null; tools return ToolResult<T>; sdk-bridge throws; withToolWrapper translates throws to typed DemosError at a single boundary. This architecture is sound and the vast majority of code follows it correctly.

add.header: withToolWrapper is reliable single error translation point
add.description: All 9 withToolWrapper-based tools converge exception handling to one place. The isDemosError duck-type check correctly preserves intentionally-thrown DemosError codes versus generic Error messages. This prevents error-code loss at the tool boundary.

add.header: Silent error swallow on Skill Dojo fallback path
add.description: scan.ts swallows the Skill Dojo fallback exception with an empty catch, losing all diagnostic context on dual-path failure. This is a concrete information-loss defect, not a design tradeoff.

add.header: JSON.parse error handling delegated to outer caller scope
add.description: discover-sources.ts loadCatalog() calls JSON.parse without local error handling, relying on the try/catch in discoverSources() at a different call stack level. This is a fragile pattern that breaks if loadCatalog is called from other contexts.

add.header: checkAndRecord* pattern in guard layer
add.description: Guards use precise compound names (checkAndRecordDedup, checkAndRecordWrite, checkAndRecordTip) that communicate atomic check+write semantics. The 'checkAndAppend' helper in state-helpers.ts follows the same pattern consistently.

add.header: Tool file/function name alignment
add.description: Every tool file is named identically to its exported function (scan.ts/scan(), pay.ts/pay(), publish.ts/publish()). This eliminates import discovery friction entirely.

add.header: Backward-compat aliases still active in guards
add.description: Three guard files export both current checkAndRecord* names and older check*/record* aliases. The aliases exist because publish.ts still uses them — they are load-bearing, not dead code.

add.header: SSRF-surfacing names in pay.ts helpers
add.description: Internal helpers like fetchWithValidatedRedirects and stripPaymentProofOnCrossOriginRedirect name the security behavior explicitly, making the security intent visible at the call site without reading the implementation.

add.header: withToolWrapper earns its indirection across 9 tools
add.description: withToolWrapper centralizes timing, session.touch(), onToolCall notification, and DemosError passthrough for all tools. It accepts a typed error code and a generic callback — no pass-through, genuine leverage.

add.header: checkAndAppend is a genuinely polymorphic generic
add.description: checkAndAppend<TState, TEntry> is used by all 5 active guards with distinct state shapes (DedupState, WriteRateState, PaySpendState, TipState). The generic type parameter represents real structural variation, not ceremonial typing.

add.header: SdkBridge interface justified as SDK isolation shell
add.description: SdkBridge has one production implementation but tests inject mocks against the interface. It explicitly isolates @kynesyslabs/demosdk volatility — a valid public API typing shell per the abstraction_fitness skip list.

add.header: Zod schemas alongside TypeScript types add runtime value
add.description: schemas.ts mirrors types.ts with Zod schemas that provide runtime validation. The compile-time sync assertions (Forward/Reverse type checks) prevent drift between the layers, making the parallel definition justified.

add.header: discoverSources bypasses withToolWrapper wrapper
add.description: discoverSources accepts DemosSession | null, so it cannot use withToolWrapper as currently typed. It manually replicates touch() and onToolCall logic, creating a coverage gap in the abstraction.

add.header: Lean 5-dep production set, no duplication
add.description: The toolkit uses exactly 5 production deps (demosdk, zod, proper-lockfile, yaml, @anthropic-ai/sdk), each serving a distinct purpose. No duplicate HTTP clients, date libraries, or utility frameworks.

add.header: Lazy SDK imports prevent module-level side effects
add.description: connect.ts and sdk-bridge.ts use dynamic import() for @kynesyslabs/demosdk to avoid module-level crypto polyfill and global state mutation. This is an intentional dependency isolation pattern.

add.header: proper-lockfile is justified for cooperative file locking
add.description: proper-lockfile provides stale TTL + exponential retry semantics for file locks that have no direct Node.js equivalent. It is not a heavy dep for light use — it is the appropriate tool for the state-store locking requirement.

add.header: yaml in production deps, unused in toolkit layer
add.description: yaml is a production dependency used in CLI/config layers (YAML persona files, strategy configs) but not within the toolkit itself. This is accurate for the monorepo scope but makes the toolkit's production dep set appear slightly broader than it needs to be.

add.header: Guards use single-responsibility closure predicate pattern
add.description: Each guard's check logic is an inline closure passed to checkAndAppend — the predicate has no side effects, is pure, and returns null (allowed) or string (error message). This keeps guard internals minimal and testable.

add.header: pay.ts 9-step pipeline is long but single-responsibility
add.description: The pay() function is 320 LOC with 9 distinct steps. Each step is logically distinct (idempotency, SSRF, spend cap, parse 402, payee check, amount guard, settle, record, retry) and labeled with step comments. The length is justified by the protocol complexity, not concern mixing.

add.header: url-validator.ts uses flat if-return chains for IP range checks
add.description: checkBlockedIpv4 covers 9 IP ranges using flat if-return conditions with informative reason strings. No nesting beyond a single level. The structure is direct and readable despite covering many ranges.

add.header: verify.ts has dead-code fallback after exhaustive retry loop
add.description: The post-loop return err() at lines 59-62 of verify.ts is never reached — all loop iterations terminate with explicit returns inside the body. The unreachable code creates misleading impression that exhaustion handling requires this path.

add.header: tip.ts feed scan for author resolution is implicit and limited
add.description: tip() resolves the post author by fetching 50 recent posts and scanning linearly. Posts older than the last 50 will fail with INVALID_INPUT. This limitation is not documented in the function signature or docstring.

add.header: withToolWrapper cleanly centralizes cross-cutting seam concerns
add.description: Timing, touch(), onToolCall, and error wrapping are extracted into a single wrapper used by 7+ tools. The seam contract is explicit: fn receives startMs, returns ToolResult<T>, wrapper handles everything else.

add.header: Guard boundary interface is minimal and uniform
add.description: All guards expose check*(store, walletAddress, ...) returning DemosError | null. This makes them composable at tool layer via parallel Promise.all or sequential checks with identical error propagation.

add.header: SDK bridge opaquely stored in signingHandle creates cast overhead
add.description: The bridge is stored as an untyped field inside signingHandle: unknown in DemosSession, requiring a runtime cast in getBridge(). This was likely a transitional decision during initial implementation that should be formalized with a typed interface.

add.header: Multi-fallback SDK response parsing localized but undocumented
add.description: publishHivePost has a 6-way txHash extraction fallback and scan.ts has a 5-way feed shape fallback. The SDK response shape variation is real but the glue is inline rather than extracted into named adapters with version documentation.

add.header: Integration test with real mock bridge exists
add.description: integration.test.ts covers the full tool layer with a typed mock SdkBridge, verifying behavioral contracts for attest, scan, react, verify, tip, publish, and reply in one file. This is genuine cross-module verification, not just unit isolation.

add.header: D402 flow has thorough targeted tests
add.description: pay-d402.test.ts mocks fetch globally and covers the complete 9-step challenge/response flow including idempotency, spend cap, payee validation, and receipt recording. The pattern (mock SSRF validator + mock bridge) is the right approach for other tools.

add.header: verify.ts retry logic tested with real sleep in CI
add.description: The integration test for verify() uses a 25s timeout, suggesting real delays run in CI. This creates slow tests and does not cover exhaustion or retry count boundaries.

add.header: Conditional integration assertions weaken failure-path coverage
add.description: attest and publish integration tests use if/else with tautological else branches when DNS fails, meaning in most CI environments these tests always exercise only the else branch and assert almost nothing.

add.header: Core tool API (session, opts) convention is consistent
add.description: All tools except discoverSources share an identical calling convention: (session: DemosSession, opts: XxxOptions) => Promise<ToolResult<XxxResult>>. Error codes are typed (DemosErrorCode), validation is uniform (validateInput at entry), and parameter count is always 2.

add.header: discoverSources is a three-way outlier on public surface
add.description: discoverSources deviates from the tool protocol in three ways: nullable session parameter, manual reimplementation of withToolWrapper boilerplate, and a dead matchThreshold option field. Together these make it the primary coherence gap in the public API.

add.header: SSRF validation uniformly applied at all external URL inputs
add.description: attest.ts, pay.ts (initial request + each redirect hop), and publish.ts all call validateUrl before any outbound request. scan/react/verify/tip use only relative API paths blocked from absolute URL injection at the bridge. No caller-controlled URL escapes SSRF validation.

add.header: Bearer token auto-attached at bridge layer, not per tool
add.description: apiCall() in sdk-bridge.ts attaches Authorization: Bearer unconditionally when authToken is not AUTH_PENDING_TOKEN. Tools never manually manage token attachment — enforced at a single boundary. Both degraded-session paths (auth failure, address-only wallet) correctly use AUTH_PENDING_TOKEN to suppress the header.

add.header: urlAllowlist is a dead security control
add.description: ConnectOptions.urlAllowlist is stored in DemosSession but never passed to validateUrl(), which has no allowlist parameter. Callers who configure urlAllowlist receive no filtering effect — the control exists in config but has zero runtime impact.

add.header: Spend cap guards consistently wallet-scoped and persistent
add.description: All spending guards (tip, pay, write-rate) scope state by wallet address via stateKey() and persist across session restarts via FileStateStore. This ensures cap enforcement is durable and consistent across reconnects.

add.header: No debug logging or generic domain names
add.description: The entire toolkit contains no console.log dumps, entry/exit trace logging, or generic names (handleData, processItem). Every identifier uses precise domain vocabulary — attestDahr, checkWriteRateLimit, withToolWrapper — making the signal-to-noise ratio high.

add.header: Restating comments in high-traffic helper
add.description: state-helpers.ts (7 importers) uses inline section headers that restate the code directly below rather than explaining business rationale. The parenthetical on the persist comment is the only exception.

add.header: Record function duplication across 4 guards
add.description: Four recordX() functions each independently implement load→prune→push→set despite checkAndAppend in state-helpers.ts providing exactly this capability. The refactor was applied to the check path but not the record path.

add.header: Atomic guard API exists but is unexported
add.description: Three guards (write-rate-limit, dedup-guard, tip-spend-cap) introduced unified checkAndRecord* functions for atomic check+record under a single lock, but only the legacy split-call API is exported from index.ts. The migration is half-done: implementation updated, public surface not.

add.header: No mixed JS/TS or stale migration debt
add.description: The codebase is 100% TypeScript with no .js companion files. No stale 'remove after', 'legacy', or 'migrate' TODO comments exist. The mechanical scan 'deprecated' signals are false positives — those symbols are actively used.

add.header: Three-layer directory mirrors responsibility boundaries
add.description: toolkit/ (contracts + session), guards/ (safety enforcement), tools/ (tool implementations) are non-overlapping concerns with unidirectional coupling. A developer can predict which directory owns a new file without reading existing code.

add.header: No reciprocal coupling or straggler root files
add.description: The coupling matrix shows no bidirectional directory edges. All 24 production files are placed under the three subdirectories with no root-level stragglers.

add.header: tool-wrapper.ts placement is marginally ambiguous
add.description: tool-wrapper.ts lives in tools/ but all 7 of its importers are other tools — making it a shared utility inside the consumer directory. Not a structural violation, but slightly harder to discover when looking for cross-cutting toolkit helpers.

add.header: Lazy SDK imports prevent module-level side effects
add.description: All Demos SDK imports use dynamic await import() inside function bodies, documented as an explicit design goal in connect.ts. No SDK state is created at module load time — each session gets a fresh SDK instance.

add.header: No module-scope env var reads
add.description: The toolkit reads no process.env variables at module scope. All environment-dependent configuration flows through ConnectOptions at runtime, making test isolation straightforward.

add.header: Two module-level caches without flush API
add.description: _isContainer (connect.ts) and catalogCache (discover-sources.ts) are module-level singletons. _isContainer is documented as process-invariant. catalogCache has no flush mechanism, making test isolation harder when different catalog paths are needed across tests.

add.header: homedir() called at state-store module scope
add.description: DEFAULT_STATE_DIR in state-store.ts captures homedir() at import time. FileStateStore accepts a dir override, so test isolation is achievable by passing a temp dir — the module-level default is rarely used in tests.

add.header: Consistent withToolWrapper pipeline pattern across all tools
add.description: Every toolkit tool follows: public function → withToolWrapper → guards → bridge calls → ok/err. This is a deliberate structural pattern that makes the control flow predictable and consistent across all 10 tools.

add.header: pay.ts sequential protocol correctly exceeds LOC threshold
add.description: The D402 challenge/response protocol has 9 sequential steps with proper helper extraction (fetchWithValidatedRedirects, withWalletSettlementLock, safeReadBody). LOC is justified by protocol complexity, not mixed concerns.

add.header: sdk-bridge.ts apiCall silent catch conflates failure types
add.description: Network exceptions are converted to { ok: false, status: 0 } which is indistinguishable from an HTTP 0-status response. Callers lose the ability to distinguish retryable network errors from non-retryable HTTP errors.

add.header: ToolResult<T> uniform return contract across all tools
add.description: All 10 tools return Promise<ToolResult<T>> with no mixed throw/return patterns within the module API. This is a strong positive contract discipline enforced by withToolWrapper.

add.header: attestUrl optional type vs required runtime mismatch
add.description: PublishDraft.attestUrl is typed optional (?) but throws at runtime if absent. This was intentionally designed as 'two-layer validation' but the type annotation communicates the wrong contract to callers who rely on TypeScript types.

add.header: verify() blockHeight hardcoded to 1 — fabricated data
add.description: When a post is found in the feed, verify() returns blockHeight: 1. The feed API does not provide block height, so this placeholder was never replaced with real data. Callers receive a typed field with meaningless content.

add.header: Control flow generally direct, no complex branching
add.description: The toolkit tools follow clear sequential patterns (validate → guard → execute → record) with no identical if/else branches or always-true conditions found across the 10 tools and 6 guards reviewed.

add.header: verify() for-loop has unreachable post-loop return
add.description: The inclusive upper bound (attempt <= RETRY_DELAYS_MS.length) means the final retry is handled inside the loop, making the post-loop error return dead code. This indicates the loop termination logic was not fully verified.

add.header: pay-spend-cap.ts validates limits before input validity
add.description: The maxPerCall check fires before the isFinite check, which means Infinity input gets SPEND_LIMIT error code instead of INVALID_INPUT. Guard ordering should validate input shape before comparing against policy.

add.header: Public API surface fully typed with no any escapes
add.description: types.ts, index.ts, and all tool function signatures use fully typed interfaces. Every option type, result type, and error type is precise. The Zod schemas include compile-time sync assertions that prevent schema/interface drift. This is the codebase's strongest type safety layer.

add.header: Zod compile-time sync assertions prevent schema drift
add.description: schemas.ts lines 173-194 use type-level assertions to verify schema output extends interface (forward) and interface extends schema input (reverse). Zero runtime cost. This is an unusually disciplined approach to keeping Zod schemas synchronized with TypeScript interfaces.

add.header: No @ts-ignore or @ts-expect-error in toolkit source
add.description: All type escapes use explicit `as` casts with localized scope — no file-level type suppression. Casts are visible and traceable to specific SDK gaps.

add.header: SDK boundary is the primary any sink — partially SDK-forced
add.description: The Demos SDK does not declare web2.createDahr(), sendTransaction(), or transfer() in its TypeScript interface, forcing as any casts at SDK call sites. The bare Function txModule parameter and any-typed cache variables are not SDK-forced and reduce type safety unnecessarily.

add.header: SourceStatus duplicated in discover-sources.ts
add.description: A local SourceStatus type definition in discover-sources.ts exactly duplicates the exported type from types.ts. This creates a silently-diverging copy that will cause incorrect normalizeStatus behavior if types.ts adds a new status value.
